summary: DeVault describes the trust-on-first-use (TOFU) algorithm he implemented for his Gemini protocol client, gmni. The post provides a detailed specification for how Gemini clients should handle certificate trust using a known_hosts file modeled after SSH. He walks through the verification steps, trust states (TRUSTED, UNTRUSTED, UNKNOWN, INVALID), and user prompts for each case. The post emphasizes that self-signed certificates are the norm in Gemini and that traditional certificate authorities should be deliberately excluded. He offers this as a recommendation for other Gemini client implementers to follow.
key_points: Gemini's specification explicitly supports TOFU and self-signed certificates, but leaves implementation details to client developers
The algorithm uses a known_hosts file (similar to SSH) storing hostname, fingerprint algorithm, fingerprint hash, and expiration timestamp
Trust states are categorized as TRUSTED, UNTRUSTED, UNKNOWN, or INVALID, each with specific user-facing actions
CA-signed certificates are given no special treatment — they are TOFU'd the same as self-signed certs
Server operators are encouraged to use self-signed certificates and explicitly avoid mainstream certificate authorities
money_quotes: Self-signed certificates are the norm in Geminiland, and if your client does not support them then you're going to be unable to browse many sites.
Being signed by a certificate authority in the system trust store is not considered meaningful to this algorithm. Such a cert is TOFU'd all the same.
You should use a self-signed certificate, and you should not use a certificate signed by one of the mainstream certificate authorities.
We don't need to carry along the legacy CA cabal into our brave new Gemini future.
If UNTRUSTED, abort the request and display a diagnostic message. The user must manually edit the known_hosts file to correct the issue.
key_insight: The Gemini protocol offers an opportunity to replace the centralized certificate authority model with a simpler, SSH-style trust-on-first-use approach that puts control back in users' hands.
